rules.name: SAM registry export
rules.description: Exporting the SAM, SYSTEM, or SECURITY registry hive which together contain Windows password hashes for all local accounts. These hashes can be cracked offline or used directly in pass-the-hash attacks.
rules.guidance: Terminate the harness immediately. SAM/SYSTEM hive export is never a legitimate coding task. Rotate all local Windows account passwords and review recent login events in Event Viewer.

rules.name: reading SSH private keys
rules.description: Accessing SSH private key files. Private keys grant passwordless authentication to remote servers, cloud instances, and code repositories. A copied key gives an attacker persistent remote access.
rules.guidance:
1. Check if the harness had a legitimate reason to read SSH keys.
2. If unexpected, rotate the affected keys immediately on all remote systems.
3. Review ~/.ssh/authorized_keys on remote servers for unauthorised entries.

rules.name: reading AWS credentials file
rules.description: Reading ~/.aws/credentials, which contains AWS access keys and secret keys for all configured profiles. These keys grant full programmatic access to AWS resources.
rules.guidance:
1. Verify the harness had a legitimate AWS task (deploy, S3 upload, etc.).
2. If unexpected, rotate the affected AWS access keys in the IAM console immediately.
3. Review AWS CloudTrail for unexpected API calls in the past 24 hours.

rules.name: mimikatz patterns
rules.description: Known mimikatz commands for in-memory credential dumping from LSASS. Mimikatz can extract plaintext passwords, NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets, and certificate private keys from Windows memory.
rules.guidance: This is an unambiguous attack tool. If your harness executed these commands, assume your system is compromised. Report to your security team immediately, change all passwords, and revoke all authentication tokens.

rules.name: LSASS process dump
rules.description: Dumping lsass.exe memory, the primary Windows credential harvesting technique. LSASS holds cached credentials, NTLM hashes, and Kerberos tickets for all logged-in users. A dump can be processed offline to extract all credentials.
rules.guidance: This is a critical security incident. Terminate all sessions, change all passwords, and report to your security team immediately. Check if Windows Credential Guard is enabled to mitigate future attacks.

rules.name: PowerShell credential extraction
rules.description: Extracting credentials from PSCredential objects or decoding SecureString — converts protected credential objects back to plaintext.
rules.guidance:
1. Review whether the harness needed to handle credentials for a legitimate task.
2. If unexpected, check what credentials were being processed and rotate them.

rules.name: Chrome/Chromium Login Data access
rules.description: Accessing or copying Chrome's 'Login Data' SQLite database, which contains encrypted usernames and passwords for all saved websites. Any process running as the same user can decrypt it — no admin rights needed.
rules.guidance:
1. Terminate the harness immediately if credential access was not explicitly requested.
2. Change your most important browser-saved passwords (banking, email, work accounts).
3. Enable 2FA on critical accounts as a protective measure.

rules.name: Firefox credential database access
rules.description: Accessing Firefox's logins.json (saved passwords) or key4.db (encryption master keys). Together these allow decryption of all stored Firefox passwords.
rules.guidance:
1. Terminate the harness immediately.
2. Set or change your Firefox master password.
3. Consider rotating credentials for sites saved in Firefox.

rules.name: Windows Credential Manager enumeration
rules.description: Enumerating or reading credentials from the Windows Credential Manager, which stores website passwords, RDP saved passwords, and application secrets. All stored credentials are accessible to processes running as the current user.
rules.guidance:
1. If you did not ask the harness to retrieve stored credentials, terminate it immediately.
2. Review Windows Credential Manager (Control Panel > Credential Manager) for sensitive entries.

rules.name: WiFi password extraction
rules.description: Extracting stored WiFi network passwords from Windows WLAN profiles. Windows stores WiFi credentials including plaintext WPA2 passwords, accessible to any non-admin user for networks they have connected to.
rules.guidance:
1. WiFi password extraction is almost never a legitimate coding task.
2. If unexpected, change the passwords for any WiFi networks you use.
3. Notify your network administrator if this was a corporate WiFi password.

rules.name: PowerShell DPAPI decryption
rules.description: Direct calls to Windows DPAPI (Data Protection API) to decrypt protected data. DPAPI is how Chrome, Edge, Outlook, and many Windows applications encrypt stored credentials. An attacker calling DPAPI can decrypt any protected secret belonging to the current user.
rules.guidance:
1. DPAPI decryption calls are almost never legitimate for AI coding tasks.
2. Terminate the harness immediately and audit what data it was attempting to decrypt.
3. Assume any DPAPI-protected secret (browser passwords, Wi-Fi passwords) may be compromised.

rules.name: Password manager database access
rules.description: Accessing a password manager database file (KeePass .kdbx, Bitwarden local vault, 1Password, Dashlane). These files contain encrypted copies of all your saved passwords. An attacker with the file can attempt offline brute-force of the master password.
rules.guidance:
1. Terminate the harness immediately.
2. Change your password manager master password immediately.
3. If the database was copied, assume all stored credentials are potentially at risk over time.
4. Enable hardware key (YubiKey) 2FA on your password manager if available.

rules.name: Environment variable credential search
rules.description: Searching environment variables for credential-related names (PASSWORD, SECRET, KEY, TOKEN, API_KEY). Developers commonly store secrets in environment variables, making them a frequent target for credential theft.
rules.guidance:
1. Review the harness's current task to confirm this was expected.
2. If unexpected, rotate any secrets you store in environment variables (API keys, tokens, passwords).

rules.name: Git credential helper access
rules.description: Accessing Git's credential store, which holds GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket authentication tokens and passwords. Credential helper 'fill' or 'get' retrieves stored credentials for a given host.
rules.guidance:
1. Verify the harness was performing a legitimate Git operation.
2. If unexpected, revoke and regenerate your GitHub/GitLab personal access tokens.

rules.name: Cloud provider credential file access
rules.description: Reading Azure access token cache, GCP application default credentials, or other cloud provider authentication files. These grant programmatic access to cloud resources and can be used to pivot into cloud infrastructure.
rules.guidance:
1. Verify the harness had a legitimate cloud deployment task.
2. If unexpected, immediately revoke the affected cloud credentials in the respective console.
3. Review cloud audit logs for suspicious API calls.

rules.name: PowerShell command history mining
rules.description: Reading PowerShell's saved command history file (ConsoleHost_history.txt). Developers frequently type API keys, passwords, and tokens directly into the PowerShell prompt, leaving them in plaintext in the history file.
rules.guidance:
1. If unexpected, review your PowerShell history file for credentials you may have typed.
2. Clear your history: Remove-Item (Get-PSReadLineOption).HistorySavePath
3. Rotate any credentials or tokens that appear in your history.

rules.name: Developer token file access
rules.description: Accessing developer authentication token files: .npmrc (npm registry tokens), .pypirc (PyPI upload credentials), .netrc (host credentials). These files contain long-lived access tokens for package registries and development services.
rules.guidance:
1. Verify whether the harness was performing a legitimate package publishing or registry task.
2. If unexpected, rotate the tokens: revoke npm tokens at npmjs.com, PyPI tokens at pypi.org.

rules.name: Active Directory credential attack
rules.description: Executing Active Directory credential extraction attacks: DCSync (replicating domain controller password hashes), Kerberoasting (requesting service tickets for offline cracking), or BloodHound domain enumeration. These are exclusively offensive security tools.
rules.guidance: This is an unambiguous attack against your Active Directory environment. Report to your security/IT team immediately, rotate all domain credentials, and investigate how the harness executed these tools.

rules.name: Lateral movement / pass-the-hash
rules.description: Using credential hashes or stolen tokens to move laterally across a network. Tools like CrackMapExec, Impacket, and PTH-toolkit are exclusively used in penetration testing and attacks — never in legitimate coding harness tasks.
rules.guidance: This represents active lateral movement in your network. Disconnect from the network immediately, report to your security team, rotate all credentials, and conduct a full incident response investigation.
